## [00:00 - 01:29]

If they all four of them came together and had a child, be me. If you all noticed the way that I speak, I was born in Australia, but I have what people would call sounds a bit more British, right? It's not British, I'm just articulating my words more. That's all, which makes me sound a little bit like I'm from Britain. So I found a speaker that I really liked, Simon Sinek, I love Simon Sinek, and I love the way he articulates. Every time he talks, it seems like something so clear and crystal and important. So I copied that. Les Brown. Oh, I love Les Brown. He fuels the words when he talks. The magician from the UK, his name is Derren Brown. They're not related. If you get the chance, YouTube him. You'll lose yourself for hours if not days. Again, not related. Brannay Brown. And Brannay Brown, she has this ability the moment she talks. You just sit there and you go, oh, want to be your friend? She's just got this wonderful quality. When it comes to communication skills, I think of it like a chef. A chef becomes a great chef by first of all, looking up by the great chefs and then they copy the ingredients. And then the second time they do it, they get a bit of confidence. Or you know what, I'm going to add some, I'm going to add a bit of extra chili. And then after that, you go, I want to copy another chef and you copy different chefs. The same method that chefs use to become great chefs, you can use to become a better communicator. You pick three to five speakers or presenters that you love, what it is about that person that you like. Imitate it. Don't be afraid to try it. And there'll be things you try and you can actually don't like that. It's okay. And that's how we come up with our own unique ingredient. It's the same way chefs become great chefs.
